file_name: Vidhyalakshmi and Kanchana - 2023 - Classification of skin disease using a novel hybrid flash butterfly optimization from dermoscopic im.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel hybrid flash butterfly optimized convolutional neural network with bidirectional long short-term memory (HFB-CNN-BiLSTM) for the classification of skin diseases (melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevus) from dermoscopic images in the Ham10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel hybrid flash butterfly optimized convolutional neural network with bidirectional long short-term memory (HFB-CNN-BiLSTM) approach. It details original experimental methods, including data preprocessing of the Ham10000 dataset, model architecture, parameter settings, and performance validation (achieving 96.3% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of skin disease using a novel hybrid flash butterfly optimization from dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel hybrid flash butterfly optimized convolutional neural network with bidirectional long short-term memory (HFB-CNN-BiLSTM) for the automatic classification of skin diseases from dermoscopic images. The model addresses class imbalance in the Ham10000 dataset by employing a preprocessing pipeline with data augmentation to increase training samples. It categorizes images into normal and abnormal classes, specifically identifying melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevus. The framework's performance was validated using MATLAB2018b and compared against existing approaches using metrics such as accuracy, recall, precision, and specificity. The proposed HFB-CNN-BiLSTM model achieved a maximum classification accuracy of 96.3% and an area under the curve (AUC) score of approximately 0.965. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach is highly superior in performance compared to other existing methods.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (melanoma, benign keratosis, melanocytic nevus, and normal/no disease)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin diseases from dermoscopic images using the Ham10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories: normal (no disease), melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevus. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, specificity, and AUC, with reported accuracies of 96.3% for benign keratosis, 96.5% for melanoma, and 95.5% for melanocytic nevus.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for the study. The dataset is noted to be highly imbalanced.

use.training: Used for training the HFB-CNN-BiLSTM model. The study employed data augmentation to balance the classes and increase training samples. Specific splits included 29,400 training images (after augmentation) for 80% split, 25,200 for 70% split, 21,000 for 60% split, and 16,800 for 50% split.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance. Specific splits included 12,600 testing images for 20% split, 16,800 for 30% split, 21,000 for 40% split, and 25,200 for 50% split.
use.other: The study focused classification experiments on three specific abnormal classes: melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevus, while other classes (AK, BCC, VL, DF) were present in the dataset but not the primary focus of the reported classification metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, recall, precision, specificity, and AUC, with comparisons made qualitatively ('highly superior') rather than statistically.

overall_performance.description: The proposed HFB-CNN-BiLSTM model achieved a maximum classification accuracy of 96.3% and an AUC score of approximately 0.965.

architecture_used.main_model: HFB-CNN-BiLSTM (Hybrid Flash Butterfly optimized Convolutional Neural Network with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory)

bilstm_details.description: Two BiLSTM layers, each with 256 stacked LSTM blocks, applied in backward and forward directions to learn bidirectional long-range dependencies.

training_details.hardware: Intel core i3 processor with 4 GB RAM and 2.10 GHz
training_details.data_augmentation: Flip-up, rotation, and scaling to balance classes and increase training samples from 10,015 to 42,000
training_details.loss_function: Minimized via HFBO optimization (Fitness function F = Minimum (Loss function))

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: HFB-CNN-BiLSTM (Hybrid Flash Butterfly optimized Convolutional Neural Network with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory)
ARL-CNN [15] (Attention Residual Learning-based Convolutional Neural Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to balance classes and increase training samples

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Vertical flip operation applied to balance classes and increase training samples.
Horizontal flip operation applied to balance classes and increase training samples.
Rotation by 90 degrees applied to balance classes and increase training samples.
The above operations were executed several times until the number of images in every class reached 6000, increasing the total dataset size from 10,015 to 42,000 images.

conclusions: The proposed HFB-CNN-BiLSTM approach is highly superior in terms of all performance metrics compared to other existing approaches.
The classification accuracy achieved by the proposed HFB-CNN-BiLSTM model in detecting three kinds of skin diseases (melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevus) is about 96.3%.
The proposed framework attained a maximum accuracy of 96.3% and an area under the curve (AUC) score of about 0.965.
Data augmentation was effective in preprocessing the highly imbalanced Ham10000 dataset to improve classification performance.
The training accuracy was observed to be higher compared to the testing accuracy.
Future work will investigate the impacts of underrepresented classes using other hybrid metaheuristic algorithms.
important_remarks: The study focuses exclusively on three classes (melanoma, benign keratosis, melanocytic nevus) from the Ham10000 dataset, ignoring other clinically relevant classes such as basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and vascular lesions, which limits the generalizability of the model to broader dermatological contexts.

The dataset was heavily augmented (flips, rotations) to balance classes, increasing the total sample size from 10,015 to 42,000; this synthetic data generation may introduce bias or overfitting risks not present in real-world clinical distributions.

The model was trained and tested on a single dataset (Ham10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about its robustness and applicability to diverse skin types or imaging devices.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed HFB-CNN-BiLSTM model over baselines; comparisons are based solely on point estimates of accuracy and other metrics.

The hardware specifications used for training (Intel Core i3, 4 GB RAM) are modest for deep learning tasks, potentially limiting the complexity of models that can be practically deployed in resource-constrained environments.

The paper claims 'highly superior' performance but does not provide detailed ablation studies or error analysis to explain why the hybrid flash butterfly optimization specifically improves upon standard CNN or BiLSTM architectures.

The confusion matrix data provided in Table 6 appears inconsistent with the per-class metrics in Table 7 (e.g., Melanoma accuracy is listed as 96.5% in Table 7 but the confusion matrix shows 84.06% for the same class), indicating potential reporting errors or misinterpretation of metrics.
